interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Northern Coordinator and Civil Rights Activist
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

main_summary.summary:
Betty Garman Robinson, born January 8, 1939 in New York City, recounts her trajectory from a Pleasantville, New York suburban upbringing to becoming the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee's (SNCC) Northern Coordinator from 1964 to 1966. Her father, a Pennsylvania-German-descended professor of physics and chemistry at NYU who later became chief scientist at General Precision Labs, brought home anguish from corporate cutthroat dynamics that shaped Robinson's early aversion to corporate America. Her mother, a working-class New Yorker of Irish and German immigrant descent, was openly prejudiced against Black people and other people of color, including the family's Mexican godmother Lucy Droz. Robinson describes pivotal episodes of her racial consciousness, including her mother weeping after a Black Vassar student counselor attended a party at their house during a YMCA summer camp, and her own arc through Skidmore College where she shifted her major from math to psychology to political science after attending the 1958 National Student Association Congress at Ohio Wesleyan.

Robinson traces her growing radicalization through the spring 1960 lunch-counter sit-ins, when she led 200 Skidmore women in a picket of the Saratoga Springs Woolworth that resulted in four students being taken to a police station and a college president invoking in loco parentis. She describes being recruited into the National Student Association's International Student Relations Seminar in Philadelphia after her 1960 graduation, an Ohio Wesleyan-style training ground that she later learned was a CIA pipeline -- with NSA's international program covertly funded by the agency through foundation conduits, students traveling overseas filing reports that flowed to the CIA, and inner-circle officers being made 'witty' (knowingly compliant). Robinson attended the 1962 World Festival of Youth and Students in Helsinki under CIA-funded auspices alongside Gloria Steinem and Harold Bakken, and after the LID-SDS split she gravitated toward SNCC because she felt affirmed there in ways the male-dominated SDS did not affirm her.

Robinson came to the Atlanta SNCC office on Raymond Street in March 1964 and served the summer of 1964 as the lead Northern Coordinator in the Greenwood, Mississippi office during Freedom Summer, where SNCC relocated its national operations as a statement of commitment to the Delta organizing. In fall 1964 she became Northern Coordinator full-time after Dinky Romilly moved to New York; in fall 1965 she shifted to federal-programs work, traveling to Arkansas projects in Little Rock and Pine Bluff to document discriminatory administration of the Agricultural Stabilization Conservation Service, food stamps, and school-desegregation freedom-of-choice plans. With Marion Barry she examined the U.S. Office of Education's files in Washington and co-authored a report -- presented at a press conference attended by John Lewis in November 1965 -- documenting federal acquiescence to minimal school-district desegregation plans. Robinson then moved to the SNCC Washington office where she worked on the Free DC campaign and a Benning Road bus-fare boycott in spring 1966 before resigning, citing the deteriorating participatory culture inside the office. She describes the Sojourner Motor Fleet of ~20-40 cars (purchased at dealer cost through United Auto Workers connections), the SNCC WATS-line telephone system, the $9.64-after-tax weekly field salary, the Howard University Thanksgiving 1963 SNCC meeting where freedom singing crystallized her commitment, and her collaborative editing of the 2010 book 'Hands on the Freedom Plow' with Judy Richardson, Dottie Zellner, Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescott Norman, and Jean Smith Young.

After leaving SNCC in 1966, Robinson worked in a Washington Black community arts program, anti-war organizing after Dr. King's 1968 death, and consciousness-raising circles in the early 1970s women's movement. In 1972 she moved to Baltimore to work in a factory for eight years, married a Black factory worker, then worked for a Hopkins-based occupational-medicine doctor under an HRSA grant, then at the Johns Hopkins School of Public Health in injury prevention and HIV/AIDS research for 14 years. From 1997 she did neighborhood organizing for the Citizens Planning and Housing Association in Baltimore, held an Open Society Institute fellowship, and now works with Showing Up for Racial Justice (SURJ) -- organizing white people to support Black-led racial-justice work alongside Black Lives Matter and locally with Baltimore Racial Justice Action. She frames the current moment as connected to the SNCC vision of multiracial democracy, while emphasizing that contemporary white organizers must work in accountability relationships with Black-led organizations and recognize the centrality of the Black freedom struggle to all transformative work in the United States.
key_themes: Northern white women's pathway into the Southern Black freedom struggle
Behind-the-scenes SNCC organizational infrastructure (WATS-line, Sojourner Motor Fleet, Northern Coordinator role)
CIA infiltration of the National Student Association's international program
Federal civil rights enforcement and discriminatory administration of programs (ASCS, food stamps, school desegregation)
Racial consciousness, white privilege, and the limits of 1960s white-ally organizing
Working-class organizing, factory work, and the long arc of post-SNCC activism
Continuity between 1960s civil rights organizing and contemporary Black Lives Matter / SURJ work
main_summary.historical_significance: Robinson's interview is unusually rich on the SNCC Atlanta office and Northern Coordinator infrastructure that sustained the field projects -- daily WATS-line check-ins, the Friends of SNCC fundraising apparatus, payroll mechanics, the Sojourner Motor Fleet, and inter-organizational coordination with NAACP LDF, SCLC, CORE, the National Lawyers Guild, and the Medical Committee for Human Rights. Her firsthand account of the CIA's covert funding of the National Student Association international program and the 'witting' inner circle provides primary-source corroboration of the history later documented in Karen Paget's 'Patriotic Betrayal' (2015), which Robinson holds up during the interview. Her account of the SNCC federal-programs documentation work with Marion Barry in 1965 fills a documented gap in scholarship on how the post-Atlantic-City SNCC translated voting-rights organizing into demands for equitable administration of New Deal- and Great Society-era federal benefits.

improvements: Robinson's father's MIT radiation-lab role is framed by Robinson herself as 'the laboratory where the atomic bomb was being constructed' but his work was on radar; summary preserves Robinson's framing accurately but reviewers may note the MIT Rad Lab is historically associated with radar (not Manhattan Project bomb construction)
The 'Walter Williams' name for the Southern Student Freedom Fund coordinator is identified by Robinson as tentative ('I think his name was Walter Williams maybe'); summary preserves the hedge
The Freddie Gray inference connecting the April 2015 Baltimore uprising to SURJ regional-resource recruitment is contextual rather than explicitly stated by Robinson; flagged in citation_audit

chapters.title: Childhood in New York and Awakening to Family Prejudice
chapters.summary:
Robinson describes being born January 8, 1939 in New York City to a father who was a physics and chemistry professor at NYU and later chief scientist at General Precision Labs in Pleasantville, New York, and a mother born in New York City to Irish and German immigrant parents who was the first in her family to attend college (NYU). She recounts moving to Cambridge, Massachusetts during World War II when her father worked at the MIT radiation laboratory on radar (which Robinson describes as 'the laboratory where the atomic bomb was being constructed,' though she notes her father worked on radar), and remembers air raids and blackout curtains shaping her later anti-war sentiments. Robinson describes her father's torment at corporate-ladder competition and her own and her brother's resulting rejection of corporate America (her brother became a veterinarian).

The chapter centers on Robinson's mother's open prejudice -- against Black people, immigrants, working-class people, and Catholics -- which created sustained contradiction with the family's own immigrant-class background. Robinson recounts hosting a YMCA day-camp party in high school where a Black Vassar student counselor attended; afterward Robinson found her mother weeping in the basement, saying 'you brought a nigger into the house.' She recounts her mother's contempt for the family's Mexican godmother Lucy Droz despite having asked her to be Betty's godmother because, as her mother later explained, 'she's the only Catholic I knew at the time.' Robinson reflects that her mother had Black classmates at Flushing High School in the 1920s and never explained the source of her racial anxiety.

improvements: Could clarify that 'where the atomic bomb was being constructed' is Robinson's own framing of MIT's wartime radiation lab role -- which is historically more associated with radar (MIT Rad Lab) than with Los Alamos bomb construction

chapters.title: Skidmore College, NSA Awakening, and the Saratoga Woolworth Picket
chapters.summary:
Robinson recounts arriving at Skidmore College in 1956 -- a school she chose to reject her mother's preferred 'hot shot women's colleges' (Smith, Vassar, Wellesley, Radcliffe) -- only to find herself shocked when her roommate brought thirty pairs of shoes. After running unsuccessfully for student-body secretary in her sophomore year, she was recruited to run for Skidmore's National Student Association (NSA) office and attended the 1958 NSA Congress at Ohio Wesleyan, which she describes as a life-changing exposure to students discussing South African apartheid, Southern segregation, and movement organizing. She changed her major from psychology (and earlier from math, which her father had pushed her toward in anticipation of the rise of computers) to political science.

After the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Robinson and other Skidmore students -- in a series of 'four very heated meetings' that she describes as her first experience with democratic debate -- decided to organize a picket of the Saratoga Springs Woolworth. Two hundred women students walked around the Woolworth, and the next day four students were taken to the police station under a statute meant for union-busting (no picketing within 100 yards). A freshman, Linda Chase, knew from her attorney father that the students did not have to give names until formally charged. The front page of the Saratoga paper carried the headline 'Skidmore [students get] more than they bargained for' with an editorial that the protest was 'unladylike.' The college president invoked in loco parentis. Robinson considered going to the founding SNCC meeting at Shaw University over spring break but was terrified of her mother's reaction.
related_events: SNCC founding meeting at Shaw University (April 1960)
National Defense Education Act loyalty oath campaign

improvements: The exact attendance figure of '200 women students' and the headline wording 'Skidmore [students get] more than they bargained for' are Robinson's recollections; could note slight uncertainty in the exact wording

chapters.title: The CIA Inside NSA: Philadelphia Seminar and Helsinki Festival
chapters.summary:
After graduating from Skidmore in 1960, Robinson was invited to the International Student Relations Seminar (ISRS), a two-month Philadelphia program of about twelve participants (mostly men) that she later understood was the training ground for future NSA officers and CIA recruitment. She describes the CIA's covert funding of NSA's international program through foundation conduits, the planting of CIA agents in the ISRS seminar itself, the post-election ritual of being made 'witting' ('w-i-t-t-i-n-g') -- after which insiders could speak openly about the agency relationship -- and the agency's strategic vetting of officer candidates for compliance with U.S. foreign-policy aims. Robinson holds up Karen Paget's book 'Patriotic Betrayal' (which she dates to 'maybe 2011, 2012, 2013'). She corroborates Tim Jenkins's account that, as a Black NSA student leader, he was steered from international vice-president into the domestic role because the CIA didn't trust him.

Robinson recounts traveling to the 1958 NSA Congress in Minnesota (where Algerian revolutionary students energized her), then to the 1961 Congress at the University of Wisconsin (which she organized as the NSA president's assistant, having moved from California to Philadelphia in November 1960). She describes attending the 1962 World Festival of Youth and Students in Helsinki under CIA-funded auspices alongside Gloria Steinem -- who Robinson states knew the funding was CIA -- and Harold Bakken (a late 1940s/early 1950s NSA president). At the festival students received free housing in exchange for written intelligence on foreign panels. Robinson witnessed Helsinki police beating no-testing-East-West demonstrators in the main square. She also recounts attending the New York LID/SDS split meeting after the festival, where she remembers Mickey Most banging his head on the table screaming 'those bloody communists.'
related_events: 1961 NSA Congress at University of Wisconsin
1962 World Festival of Youth and Students (Helsinki)

improvements: Robinson appears to slightly conflate the dating of the Algerian Revolution -- she says 'I don't know the dates' -- but does not assert specific dates herself

chapters.title: Choosing SNCC Over SDS and Joining the Atlanta Office
chapters.summary:
Robinson explains why she gravitated to SNCC rather than continuing with Students for a Democratic Society (SDS) after her time at UC Berkeley: in SDS she felt sidelined as a woman, 'in awe of the men and their intellect,' whereas SNCC's James Forman and Casey Hayden affirmed her capacity to take initiative. She recalls attending the SNCC Thanksgiving 1963 meeting at Howard University -- where the singing of freedom songs and a circle of people in tears singing 'We Shall Overcome' provided what she describes as her emotional connection to the movement.

She describes the structural sexism in SDS, where women were often partners of male leaders rather than independent organizers. Casey Hayden's transition from SDS to SNCC, Robinson believes, reflected both Hayden's Southern background fitting SNCC's perspective and the breakdown of her relationship with Tom Hayden. Robinson also describes the Southern Student Freedom Fund -- jointly organized by NSA, Young Christian Students, SDS, and the Northern Student Movement -- to raise scholarship money for students expelled for sit-ins, bail money, and direct support to SNCC field secretaries; she identifies Tim Jenkins and a 'Walter Williams' (possibly a Jackson State student who was expelled) as central to that fund's coordination. Robinson came to SNCC in March 1964 and worked alongside Northern Coordinator Dinky Romilly. She describes the Atlanta SNCC office as dedicated and family-like, where the support staff included Wilson Brown and Willie McCray running the printing press and producing 'The Student Voice' newspaper.
related_events: SNCC Thanksgiving 1963 meeting at Howard University

improvements: Robinson identifies the Southern Student Freedom Fund coordinator only tentatively as 'Walter Williams' -- summary preserves her hedging

chapters.title: SNCC Atlanta Operations and the Northern Coordinator Role
chapters.summary:
Robinson describes the daily mechanics of the SNCC Atlanta office on Raymond Street. Each morning and evening someone called every SNCC project via SNCC's WATS line (wide-area telephone line, effectively a 1960s 800-number) to document overnight events; field offices called collect, gave a fake operator number, and the Atlanta office would dial back through the WATS line to avoid phone-company charges. Field staff earned $10 a week -- $9.64 after taxes -- with housing usually provided by freedom houses or local families; Atlanta-based staff earned $40 a week to cover urban rent and utilities. Cigarettes were 15-20 cents a pack at the time, allowing the field stipend to cover basic subsistence.

As Northern Coordinator (a role she assumed full-time in fall 1964 after Dinky Romilly moved to New York), Robinson wrote handwritten thank-you notes to every donor, coordinated the network of Friends of SNCC offices in Northern cities, distributed daily field reports, and orchestrated coordinated action requests (calls to congressmen, the White House, the Justice Department, the FBI; federal-building rallies). The Atlanta office also distributed money to field projects, documented daily atrocities for legal use by the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, SCLC, CORE, the National Lawyers Guild, and the Medical Committee for Human Rights, and coordinated with the National Council of Churches, the Unitarians, Episcopalians, and the United Church of Christ. Robinson describes fundraising-letter writing parties with envelope-stuffing, pizza, and music. SNCC's fundraising appeals avoided pity framing ('help the poor Mississippi person') in favor of democratic-principles framing -- 'this is a democratic country, we need to be committed to our ideals.'
related_events: Atlanta SNCC office at Raymond Street

chapters.title: Federal Programs Work, MFDP Aftermath, and Black Belt Soul-Searching
chapters.summary:
In fall 1965, Robinson transitioned out of Northern Coordinator and proposed taking on federal-programs documentation work, leveraging what she acknowledges was her white-staff and educational privilege within SNCC's loose structure. She traveled to SNCC projects in Little Rock and Pine Bluff, Arkansas, taking affidavits documenting discriminatory administration of federal programs -- the Agricultural Stabilization Conservation Service (ASCS), food stamps, and the 1964 Civil Rights Act's school-desegregation 'freedom of choice' enforcement. With Marion Barry (head of the SNCC Washington office at that time), she examined the U.S. Office of Education's case files in Washington and found that congressmen would write requesting dispensation from desegregation requirements, and the office would write back relieving school districts of compliance burden. Their findings were published as a mimeographed report; John Lewis attended their press conference in November 1965.

Robinson recounts SNCC's post-Atlantic City soul-searching. After the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge was rejected at the 1964 Democratic National Convention, James Forman proposed a Black Belt Summer Project for 1965 across Alabama, Arkansas, Georgia, and South Carolina (with Jack Minnis having researched voting-denial patterns across the Black Belt counties). The project was never resolved; some volunteers did come South in summer 1965 to Mississippi. She describes the four SNCC meetings that year debating organizational direction. She recounts the March 1965 Selma campaign, the SNCC pushback against Dr. King 'plonking himself into a community,' and Amelia Boynton's actual invitation to King because she believed local pace was insufficient. From Atlanta during the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack, Robinson chartered a plane carrying Ivanhoe Donaldson, Courtland Cox, Prathia Hall, and a fourth person to Selma to back up John Lewis and SNCC field secretary John Love.
related_events: 1964 Atlantic City Democratic National Convention (MFDP Challenge)

improvements: Robinson hedges on details such as fleet size ('40 cars or 20 cars') and exact dates; summary preserves these hedges

chapters.title: Waveland, Women's Position Paper, and SNCC Internal Debates
chapters.summary:
Robinson describes the Sojourner Motor Fleet of approximately 20 to 40 cars equipped with CB radios that SNCC purchased at dealer cost through United Auto Workers connections; each project was assigned a vehicle to enable communication in dangerous areas. She speculates Harry Belafonte was a likely funding source -- Belafonte was a major benefactor to both SNCC and SCLC -- and notes that the history of money flows into SNCC, SCLC, NAACP, and CORE has never been adequately documented. She recalls Ella Baker's insistence (the 'Miss Baker' of the movement) that SNCC pursue both voter registration and direct action when major foundations offered Voter Education Project money for voting-rights work alone, fearing it would soften the organizing.

Robinson recounts the fall 1964 Waveland meeting where staff submitted position papers on every topic: structure, culture, creativity, the Freedom Democratic Party, the question of education and respect for the wisdom of sharecroppers. She places herself in the 'structure faction' arguing for stronger central organization, partly because field staff in Georgia, Alabama, and Arkansas felt like 'stepchildren' compared to Mississippi's funding and volunteer concentration. She acknowledges she was not in the women's-liberation space in 1964 -- the position paper on women (later attributed to Mary King and Casey Hayden) did not engage her at the time; she did not enter feminist consciousness until the early 1970s. She felt SNCC affirmed her sufficiently that she did not perceive gender oppression there as urgent compared to the Black freedom struggle.

chapters.title: Washington SNCC Office: Free DC and the Bus Boycott
chapters.summary:
After her federal-programs work, Robinson transitioned in late 1965 to full-time work in the SNCC Washington, D.C. office (located on Rhode Island Avenue), where she shared an apartment on California Street. Two initiatives consumed the office in early 1966: Free DC (challenging the District of Columbia's lack of congressional voting representation, with a chain-and-pulling-hands logo) and a Benning Road bus-fare boycott. Robinson recalls that an older white man named LD Pratt from Kansas or Iowa came to work closely with Marion Barry, which created internal tension because Pratt was significantly older than the staff and Barry's chumminess with him excluded the rest of the staff from decision-making.

The Free DC strategy involved approaching DC department stores (Woodward & Lothrop, Hecht Company) to display the Free DC decal in their windows, with implicit boycott threats; Robinson does not recall whether SNCC actually boycotted any business. The bus boycott was around fare increases. Robinson describes the office basement filled with telephones to coordinate the alternative ride system on Benning Road; she remembers the boycott lasted only a few days and she is uncertain whether SNCC achieved its fare-stabilization goal. Shortly after, Robinson submitted a resignation letter citing the office's deteriorating participatory culture. She notes Marion Barry has since passed, but Tina Smith (a Washington SNCC administrator) and Eric Jones could provide more detail on this history.

improvements: Robinson explicitly notes uncertainty about the boycott outcome ('I don't know if we even won the fair stabilization'); summary preserves this hedge

chapters.title: Racial Dynamics in SNCC and the Late-Movement Tensions
chapters.summary:
Robinson reflects on the racial dynamics within SNCC's interracial Atlanta office, which was predominantly Black but included white staff. She describes growing awareness in fall 1964 of all-Black parties and interracial-relationship controversies, including those around James Forman, who was in an interracial relationship and had a Black wife. She acknowledges feeling that no one within SNCC was actively 'schooling' white staff on racial privilege, microaggressions, or stereotypes -- a contrast she draws with contemporary white-anti-racist organizing. She recounts how, during the summer of 1964 in Mississippi, a hosting family gave her their only bedroom (an instance of disproportionate hospitality she now regrets accepting).

She describes the Waveland-era 'backsliding' conversations about Black SNCC men in relationships with white women -- a category that included Marion Barry (with whom Robinson herself was then in a relationship) and Stokely Carmichael. She frames these tensions as part of the rising nationalist consciousness and frustration with the country's slow pace of change. Robinson recounts being struck in the face on 18th Street in DC by a Black child after Dr. King's 1968 assassination, who called her 'you dirty white lady' -- an encounter she describes as making the structure of racial oppression viscerally clear without making her feel personally targeted as a culprit.

She also describes SNCC's discussions about whites organizing in white communities. Some white SNCC workers attempted this during the fall 1964 Mississippi elections but faced harassment from local whites; the Southern Student Organizing Committee (SSOC) was the parallel project on white Southern campuses. Robinson identifies several reasons SNCC whites did not pursue white-community organizing as a primary strategy: lack of connection to white working-class neighborhoods, the felt power of the SNCC interracial community itself, and the prevailing belief that the relevant white population was either Klan members or Klan sympathizers.
related_events: 1968 Washington DC riots after Dr. King assassination

chapters.title: SURJ, Highlander, and Contemporary Racial-Justice Work
chapters.summary:
Robinson describes her current involvement with Showing Up for Racial Justice (SURJ), a national organization of white people calling other white people into support for racial justice and accountability with Black-led organizations. SURJ formed in 2009 in response to attacks on President Obama; the organization grew through the Trayvon Martin (killed by George Zimmerman) and Michael Brown (killed in Ferguson) movements and now works with Black Lives Matter chapters. Robinson credits Michelle Alexander's 'The New Jim Crow' for shifting consciousness about mass incarceration and the criminal-justice system's role in over-policing Black communities.

In April after the Baltimore uprising following Freddie Gray's death, Robinson was asked by the national SURJ coordinator to find Baltimore voices for a 700-person national conference call, after which she helped organize SURJ door-knocking with Black Lives Matter signs in white neighborhoods (modeled on similar work in St. Louis and Arizona). She became a SURJ regional resource person and attended the Highlander Research and Education Center in August (which she frames as fulfilling a SNCC-era aspiration she had never realized -- 'I never went there during civil rights move because I wasn't important enough'). She describes Highlander's role from the 1930s tobacco/textile worker organizing through the citizen-education trainings of Septima Clark and Rosa Parks under Myles Horton, and notes Highlander was forced to relocate from its original Tennessee site after state harassment.
related_events: Baltimore uprising (April 2015) following Freddie Gray's death

improvements: Robinson acknowledges uncertainty about whether Highlander was bombed ('maybe they weren't bombed') -- summary preserves the state-harassment framing rather than asserting bombing

chapters.title: From SNCC to Baltimore: Factory Work, Hopkins, and Neighborhood Organizing
chapters.summary:
After leaving SNCC in 1966 Robinson moved to Washington, DC, where she worked in a Black community arts program for several years, participated in the anti-war movement (reconnecting with former SDS contacts), and helped distribute supplies in Black neighborhoods after the rebellion following Dr. King's 1968 assassination. She helped form an organization called 'the emergency' to organize white people on race and equity, and entered consciousness-raising groups in the early 1970s women's movement, working through her own gendered experience by being in community with women in the liberation movement.

In 1972 Robinson moved to Baltimore to work in a factory for eight years -- part of a generational turn by young radicals to ground organizing in the working class. She married a Black factory worker. When one of her daughters had a birth defect that required her to leave the factory, she took a position with an occupational-medicine doctor on an HRSA grant (a federal program with offices in Hyattsville/Rockville, Maryland) teaching medical students about workplace health, leading factory tours to Bethlehem Steel, the Baltimore Chevrolet plant, the glass plant, and the Allegheny Coast Steel facility. After the Reagan administration cut the grant program in 1981, she worked for the same doctor on an asbestos-screening project for building-trades workers. She then spent 14 years at the Johns Hopkins School of Public Health on injury-prevention and HIV/AIDS research, before transitioning to neighborhood organizing work with the Citizens Planning and Housing Association in 1997 (the same neighborhoods later depicted in HBO's 'The Wire') for eight years, then an Open Society Institute fellowship to build cross-issue, cross-constituency organizer networks. She also did consulting on community-college curricula to grow organizers of color, citing the predominance of white organizers in faith-based networks even when those networks organized in Black communities.
keywords: johns hopkins school of public health
related_events: 1968 Washington DC rebellion after Dr. King's assassination
Reagan administration cuts to occupational-medicine grants (1981)

improvements: Robinson mentions 'our coast steel' which is uncertain (could be Allegheny Coast or another firm); summary uses 'Allegheny Coast Steel' but this is approximate

chapters.title: Reflections: Movement Windows, Resources, and the Centrality of the Black Freedom Struggle
chapters.summary:
Closing the interview, Robinson offers reflections on what she believes audiences should understand about the civil rights movement and contemporary organizing. She emphasizes that movement windows are time-limited and require strategic discipline -- 'we need to go fast, but we also need to go slow to go fast' -- and frames the current Black Lives Matter movement as similar in energy and depth of commitment to the 1960s civil rights movement, but distinct in form and adapted to contemporary issues.

She stresses that movements need resources and that the white community has resources it should commit to sustaining Black-led organizations. She describes her own monthly sustainer giving to SURJ, the Baltimore Algebra Project (a youth math-tutoring and organizing organization), and ONE DC (Organizing Neighborhood Equity, which organizes against gentrification in Washington Black communities). She emphasizes the importance of scholarship -- naming Emilye Crosby (the interviewer) and Hasan Kwame Jeffries as young scholars writing important movement history -- and the importance of reading and studying history rather than relying on internet snippets. She frames the Black movement as central to all transformative work in the United States 'because the country was birthed in such an exploitative relationship to the enslaved population and to slavery as the basis of the U.S. wealth.'

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Northern Coordinator and Civil Rights Activist
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

evidence: Clear chronological arc from 1939 birth through 2015 SURJ work (Segments 9-12, 00:00:40,000 to 00:01:00,000)
Self-consciously reflective transitions: 'And we'll jump forward ahead, you know, some of my feelings' (Segment 54, 00:04:42,000)
Interviewer Crosby provides structural scaffolding: 'You started to describe your living situation in Atlanta' (Segment 642, 01:26:00,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Robinson's narrative is exceptionally well-structured chronologically with strong topical coherence within each phase of her life. She consistently signals transitions and is comfortable backtracking to fill gaps (e.g., 'But we do have to go back and do Southern Student Freedom Fund'). Interviewer Crosby is an active collaborator who helps maintain structure across the interview's significant length.

most_vivid_moment.quote: She's in tears. And I'm like, well, what the heck is going on? And she's like, you will never live us down. You brought a nigger into the house.

evidence: Exact roommate detail: 'my roommate brought in 30 pairs of shoes... 1956' (Segment 85, 00:09:48,000)
Specific WATS-line operational detail: 'we paid, I don't know, $400 a month' for the WATS line and the operator-number callback workaround (Segments 522-534, 01:21:17,000)
Precise field-staff pay: '$10 a week or $9.64 after taxes' (Segment 694, 01:30:01,680)
Specific 18th Street DC 1968 incident with child striking her after King's death (Segment 1311, 02:16:51,800)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Robinson provides exceptional specificity throughout -- full names, specific years, exact street addresses (Raymond Street, California Street, Rhode Island Avenue, Benning Road), precise dollar amounts ($9.64 weekly, $40 weekly Atlanta pay, $400 WATS-line cost), and reconstructed dialogue. The level of organizational detail is unusual for an oral history and reflects her central role in SNCC's operational infrastructure.

most_authentic_moment.quote: I considered going to Shaw to be at that founding meeting. But I was terrified of my mother's response... she told me don't show up in the house until after dark because she didn't want her and she was having some kind of women over

vulnerability_elements: Admitting to having accepted a host family's only bedroom in 1964 Mississippi without recognizing it as imposing on them
Acknowledging she was not in the women's liberation space at Waveland and dismissed the position paper at the time
Honestly framing her white-staff privilege and education privilege within SNCC
emotional_authenticity.justification: Robinson is consistently honest about her own blind spots and limitations, including her late entry into feminist consciousness and her now-regretted acceptance of disproportionate hospitality in Mississippi. Her emotional range is wide, particularly around the freedom singing at Howard 1963 and her mother's prejudice. She is less emotionally raw than some Movement interviewees, partly because she is reflecting from a posture of decades of organizing experience.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Robinson's narrative quality is excellent -- structurally coherent, exceptionally specific, and emotionally authentic. Her unique position as a Northern white woman who staffed SNCC's operational infrastructure produces unusually rich detail about organizational mechanics that more field-oriented interviewees do not capture. She is comfortable with self-criticism and consistently distinguishes what she knew at the time from what she understands in retrospect.

major_events: SNCC Atlanta office Northern Coordinator role, 1964-66 (entire central section)
Mississippi Freedom Summer 1964 at the Greenwood SNCC office (Segments 470-622)
1965 Selma response from Atlanta -- chartered plane carrying Donaldson, Cox, Hall (Segments 1064-1071)
Waveland Conference 1964 -- position papers and structure debate (Segments 1183-1220)
1962 World Festival of Youth and Students in Helsinki under CIA-funded auspices (Segments 363-401)
Free DC and Benning Road bus boycott Spring 1966 (Segments 1268-1284)

key_figures.name: James Forman
key_figures.relationship: SNCC Executive Secretary and direct supervisor

key_figures.name: Marion Barry
key_figures.relationship: SNCC Washington office head and intimate partner

key_figures.name: Casey Hayden
key_figures.relationship: SDS-to-SNCC mentor who affirmed her recruitment

key_figures.name: Dinky Romilly (Constance Romilly)
key_figures.relationship: Predecessor as Northern Coordinator; close colleague

key_figures.name: Tim Jenkins
key_figures.relationship: NSA-era colleague and Southern Student Freedom Fund collaborator

key_figures.name: Judy Richardson
key_figures.relationship: SNCC colleague and 'Hands on the Freedom Plow' co-editor

organizational_involvement.role: Skidmore campus officer, NSA Congress organizer, assistant to NSA president

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Robinson is one of the few surviving witnesses to the inside operational mechanics of SNCC's Atlanta national office during 1964-66, including the WATS-line communications system, the Sojourner Motor Fleet acquisition (purchased at dealer cost via United Auto Workers connections), the daily Friends of SNCC coordination, and the federal-programs documentation work she conducted with Marion Barry in the U.S. Office of Education files. Her firsthand corroboration of the CIA's covert NSA funding -- the 'witting' inner circle, the foundation conduits, the Helsinki festival intelligence reporting -- is primary-source material on a major Cold War intelligence operation.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Robinson held a central operational role in SNCC's national infrastructure and had close working relationships with the entire SNCC leadership cohort. Her perspective on the Atlanta office mechanics and federal-programs work is among the most detailed in the corpus and complements the more field-oriented testimony of organizers in Mississippi, Alabama, and Arkansas.

precision_examples: Exact field-staff pay: '$10 a week or $9.64 after taxes' (Segment 694, 01:30:01,680)
Specific federal-programs report timing: 'November 65, maybe where John Lewis came' (Segment 979, 01:47:48,000)
Specific 1962 Helsinki festival: 'WFDY' acronym and Gloria Steinem name (Segments 363-364, 01:05:50,800)
verifiable_claims: She moved to Atlanta SNCC in March 1964 -- corroborated by other SNCC-era testimony
She co-edited 'Hands on the Freedom Plow' (2010) with Judy Richardson, Dottie Zellner, Faith Holsaert, Martha Prescott Norman, and Jean Smith Young
Marion Barry led the SNCC Washington office in 1966 (corroborated by ground-truth corpus)
Karen Paget's 'Patriotic Betrayal' (Yale University Press, 2015) documents the NSA-CIA relationship Robinson describes
specificity_of_details.justification: Robinson provides exceptional precision for an interview of this length. She gives full names, specific years, and exact organizational details. She is appropriately honest when uncertain (e.g., 'I don't know if it was 40 cars or 20 cars'), which preserves the verifiability of confident claims.

new_information: Operational detail of SNCC's WATS-line collect-call workaround using operator numbers
Specific dollar amounts and mechanics of SNCC field-staff and office-staff pay differential
Federal-programs documentation work in 1965-66 -- a relatively underdocumented post-MFDP SNCC focus
Free DC and Benning Road bus boycott as a SNCC Washington office initiative
First-person corroboration of the CIA's 'witting' protocol for NSA officers
understudied_aspects: Northern white women's pathway into SNCC through NSA networks
SNCC's organizational mechanics (vs. its field organizing)
Post-Atlantic City SNCC strategic pivot toward federal-programs enforcement
History of money flows into SNCC, SCLC, NAACP, and CORE (which Robinson herself flags as undocumented)
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Robinson's testimony complicates the dominant narrative of SNCC's post-MFDP trajectory by documenting concrete federal-programs work (ASCS, food stamps, school desegregation enforcement) that ran in parallel to the Black Belt Summer Project debate. Her account of the Free DC and Benning Road bus boycott shows the SNCC Washington office attempting urban Northern organizing concurrent with continued Southern work -- not a clean handoff from civil rights to Black Power.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of SNCC's operational infrastructure (rather than its field organizing) will find this interview unusually valuable. Robinson is a key surviving witness to the Atlanta office mechanics and federal-programs work, and her account of CIA-NSA infiltration provides Movement-veteran corroboration of the Paget scholarship.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Robinson's interview makes substantial new contributions to several underdocumented aspects of SNCC history. Her sustained engagement with organizational mechanics, federal-programs enforcement, and CIA-NSA relations is distinctive in the SNCC oral history corpus.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Historical value is very high. Robinson is a central witness to SNCC's national-office operations during its most active period, and her testimony fills documented gaps in scholarship on SNCC's organizational infrastructure, federal-programs work, and the post-MFDP strategic pivot. Her firsthand account of CIA-NSA infiltration is primary-source material for Cold War intelligence history.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Robinson describes a clear arc from suburban-bourgeois daughter of a prejudiced mother, through college political-science awakening, to SNCC operational lead, to lifelong organizer culminating in contemporary white-anti-racist work with SURJ. Her transformation has multiple inflection points: the 1958 NSA Congress, the 1960 Saratoga Springs Woolworth picket, the 1963 Howard SNCC singing circle, and the 1972 move to Baltimore factory work.
key_turning_points: 1958 NSA Congress at Ohio Wesleyan -- politicization (Segment 130, 00:19:09,000)
Spring 1960 Saratoga Woolworth picket -- arrest experience (Segments 169-179, 00:26:21,000)
Thanksgiving 1963 SNCC Howard meeting -- freedom singing (Segments 351-355, 01:02:51,800)
Late 1965 federal-programs work with Marion Barry -- federal enforcement focus (Segments 957-987, 01:46:00,000)
1972 move to Baltimore -- working-class organizing turn (Segment 1387, 02:33:30,800)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Robinson's pre-NSA self was a math/psychology major resistant to her mother's pressure to attend elite women's colleges; her post-SNCC self is a lifelong organizer who has worked in factory floors, occupational medicine, public health research, neighborhood organizing, and contemporary white-anti-racist solidarity work. Her organizing identity has been continuous across six decades.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Robinson's arc is inspiring in its sustained commitment over nearly 60 years; her continued contemporary involvement with SURJ and her monthly sustainer giving to Black-led organizations model a long-arc organizer life that does not retreat into nostalgia.
transformation_growth.justification: Robinson's transformation arc is clear and well-articulated, with multiple identifiable turning points she narrates in real time. The arc is unusually long (six decades) and continuous, which adds historical weight.

peak_moments.description: Mother's racist confrontation after Black Vassar student attended YMCA party at the Robinson home
peak_moments.stakes: Robinson's emerging racial consciousness vs. family loyalty

peak_moments.description: Saratoga Springs Woolworth picket and arrest of four Skidmore students
peak_moments.stakes: First experience of state suppression of First Amendment rights

peak_moments.description: Chartering a plane from Atlanta to Selma in response to the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack
peak_moments.stakes: SNCC organizational mobilization in a moment of acute crisis

peak_moments.description: Being struck in the face on 18th Street DC by a Black child after Dr. King's 1968 assassination

conflict_types: Family conflict (mother's prejudice vs. Robinson's emerging anti-racism)
Institutional conflict (Skidmore president vs. picketing students)
Intra-organizational conflict (SNCC Washington office culture deterioration)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From terror (mother's reaction to Shaw founding meeting) to exhilaration (Algerian students in Minnesota, freedom singing at Howard) to grief (1968 DC rebellion) to long-arc commitment (six decades of organizing)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Robinson recounts several genuinely tense moments and one violent personal encounter (1968 DC), but her overall narrative posture is reflective rather than dramatic. The 'peak moments' are real but framed analytically rather than viscerally. Her organizational role meant she was less directly in physical danger than field-staff colleagues.

universal_themes: Daughter rebelling against parents' prejudice and expectations
Finding belonging in a community that affirms one's worth
Long-arc commitment across life stages and changing political moments
Recognizing one's own privilege and blind spots over time
The pull between safe office work and dangerous field work
Working-class identification and the cost of corporate life
relatable_struggles: Navigating parental opposition to one's vocation
Choosing between affirmation in a meaningful role and personal safety
Recognizing the disproportionate burden hosts bore for staffers
Balancing political work with personal relationships and family
human_moments: Robinson and brother bringing the Vassar student counselor to a party at age 17 (Segment 89, 00:10:25,000)
Robinson's parents sending an air conditioner to Greenwood despite opposing her presence there (Segments 623-625, 01:24:39,000)
The fundraising-letter-stuffing parties with pizza, music, and conversation (Segments 849-855, 01:42:17,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Robinson's experience of mother-daughter racial conflict, of finding belonging in a movement community, and of long-arc commitment despite political setbacks resonates broadly across audiences. Her acknowledgment of her own blind spots about privilege models the kind of self-reflection contemporary anti-racist organizers seek to cultivate.
relatable_experiences.justification: Robinson's story is highly relatable for diverse audiences. The mother-daughter prejudice conflict, the formation of meaningful community in college and SNCC, and the long-arc organizer trajectory are deeply human. Her parents' contradictory air-conditioner gesture is a small, perfect moment of family complexity.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Emotional resonance is strong. Robinson's transformation arc is clear, her conflicts with family and institutions are well-rendered, and her experience translates well across audiences. The interview is more reflective than dramatic, but the relatable human texture (especially the family conflict and the SNCC community belonging) carries emotional weight.

barriers: Acronym density (NSA, SNCC, SDS, ISRS, WFDY, LID, MFDP, ASCS, SSOC, HRSA, OSI, SURJ) without consistent expansion
Insider knowledge assumed for SNCC organizational details (Wavelands, Atlantic City challenge, Black Belt Summer Project)
References to historical figures (Jack Minnis, LD Pratt, Dinky Romilly) without contextual introduction
language_clarity.justification: Robinson is highly articulate with clear, accessible language. Her primary barrier for general audiences is acronym density and her assumption of SNCC-organizational knowledge. She self-contextualizes some references (e.g., explaining the WATS line, expanding ASCS) but not others.

high_energy_sections: Saratoga Woolworth picket sequence (Segments 165-180, 00:25:05,000-00:29:51,000) -- vivid dramatic narrative
slow_sections: Childhood family material extensively retold in second tape (Segments 277-380) -- duplicates earlier content
NSA officer-election mechanics (Segments 267-279, 00:46:04,000-00:47:24,000) can drag for non-NSA-familiar listeners
Marion Barry / LD Pratt internal-tension discussion lacks dramatic resolution (Segments 1269-1285)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: The interview is long (2h44m) and would be challenging for general audiences to sustain attention throughout. Chapter-based editing and the strong dramatic sequences (Saratoga, Howard singing, 1968 DC) could anchor shorter excerpts. The duplicate-restart at Segment 277 (where the introduction repeats due to a tape change) is a structural artifact that would need editorial attention.
narrative_flow.justification: Narrative flow is good overall but is challenged by length, occasional acronym density, and the apparent tape-side-change that duplicates early material at Segment 277. Interviewer Crosby is skilled at maintaining momentum through transitions.

themes_connected: Civil rights movement to contemporary Black Lives Matter
1960s anti-war organizing to ongoing anti-militarism work
Federal civil rights enforcement to current racial-justice policy work
1970s working-class radicalism to contemporary labor and organizing work
Cold War CIA-NSA infiltration to ongoing questions about state surveillance
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Robinson explicitly connects 1960s organizing to contemporary movements through SURJ, Black Lives Matter, and the Baltimore uprising following Freddie Gray's death. She emphasizes that contemporary white organizers must work in accountability relationships with Black-led organizations and that movements need sustained resources and scholarship.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Robinson is explicitly reflective about historical significance throughout. Her closing reflections on movement windows, the centrality of the Black freedom struggle, and the importance of long-arc commitment frame the interview as a transmission of organizer wisdom across generations.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Robinson makes strong thematic connections, especially in the closing chapters, but the connections sometimes assume audience familiarity with contemporary organizations (SURJ, Baltimore Algebra Project, ONE DC). Her meaning-making is consistent and analytically clear.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Accessibility is moderate-to-good. Robinson is highly articulate and self-contextualizes some technical references, but acronym density and the interview's length limit accessibility for general audiences. The duplicate-restart at Segment 277 is a structural artifact requiring editorial attention before public dissemination.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Betty Garman Robinson's interview is an exceptional addition to the Civil Rights History Project corpus. Her role as SNCC Northern Coordinator from 1964 to 1966 places her at the operational center of the Atlanta national office during the movement's most active period, and her testimony fills documented gaps in scholarship on SNCC's infrastructure, federal-programs enforcement work, and the CIA's covert funding of the National Student Association. The interview's narrative quality, historical value, and emotional resonance are all high; accessibility is limited primarily by length and acronym density. The duplicate-restart at Segment 277 (apparently a tape-side change) is a structural artifact requiring editorial attention before public dissemination.
key_strengths: Unique operational perspective on SNCC's national office mechanics (WATS line, Sojourner Motor Fleet, Friends of SNCC network, federal-programs documentation) that complements field-organizer testimony in the corpus
First-person corroboration of the CIA's covert NSA funding and 'witting' inner-circle protocol -- primary-source material on a major Cold War intelligence operation
Clear long-arc transformation narrative spanning six decades from 1939 birth through 2015 SURJ work, with sustained organizing identity continuous across multiple political moments
key_limitations: Acronym density (NSA, SNCC, SDS, ISRS, WFDY, LID, MFDP, ASCS, SSOC, HRSA, OSI, SURJ) limits accessibility for general audiences without supplemental context
Apparent tape-side change at Segment 277 creates a substantial duplication of childhood material that requires editorial attention before public dissemination

claims.claim: Betty Garman Robinson was born January 8, 1939 in New York City
claims.supporting_excerpt: Sure, I was born on January 8, 1939. And I was born in New York City. (Segments 9-10)

claims.claim: Her father was a physics and chemistry professor at NYU who later became chief scientist at General Precision Labs in Pleasantville, New York
claims.supporting_excerpt: he was a professor of physics and chemistry at New York University... we moved to a suburb Pleasantville, New York... He was kind of the chief scientist of a, it was called a general precision equipment, or general precision GPL. (Segments 13, 47-48)

claims.claim: Robinson's family moved to Cambridge, Massachusetts during World War II, where her father worked at the MIT radiation laboratory
claims.supporting_excerpt: my father moved, we moved to Cambridge, Massachusetts, and my father worked at MIT in the radiation lab (Segment 33)

claims.claim: Her mother was openly prejudiced against Black people and other people of color, including the family's Mexican godmother Lucy Droz
claims.supporting_excerpt: my mother was very prejudiced and very, very anti-black, anti- Anybody of color (Segment 59); she would also criticize Lucy, my godmother, she would say she speaks broken English (Segment 116)

claims.claim: A Black Vassar student counselor attended a YMCA day-camp party at the Robinson home in high school, after which Robinson's mother wept saying 'you brought a nigger into the house'
claims.supporting_excerpt: here we have an African American woman vassar student on the staff of the YMCA day camp... she's in tears. And I'm like, well, what the heck is going on? And she's like, you will never live us down. You brought a nigger into the house. (Segments 88, 92)

claims.claim: Robinson attended Skidmore College starting in 1956
claims.supporting_excerpt: I picked Skidmore College College... my roommate brought in 30 pairs of shoes. And this is in 1956 (Segments 84-85)

claims.claim: She attended the 1958 National Student Association Congress at Ohio Wesleyan
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was allowed to go or give an opportunity to go to Ohio Wesley on that summer to the National Student Congress... So, 58, this was the 1958 student congress (Segments 130, 135)

claims.claim: After the February 1960 sit-ins, Robinson led approximately 200 Skidmore women in a picket of the Saratoga Springs Woolworth
claims.supporting_excerpt: we took 200 women students from campus down to the Woolworths, walked around the Woolworths and came back to campus (Segment 169)
claims.rationale: Directly supported. Robinson herself states the figure of 200.

claims.claim: The day after the Skidmore march, four students were taken to the police station for picketing under a statute meant for union-busting
claims.supporting_excerpt: the first four students that went got picked up by the police, take into the police station... charge the students with a union with a statute that was meant for union busting basically no picketing within a hundred yards of something or other (Segments 171, 173)

claims.claim: Robinson considered going to the founding SNCC meeting at Shaw University but was terrified of her mother's reaction
claims.supporting_excerpt: I considered going to Shaw to be at that founding meeting. But I was terrified of my mother's response (Segment 182)

claims.claim: After 1960 graduation she was invited to the International Student Relations Seminar (ISRS) in Philadelphia, which she later learned was a CIA-funded NSA officer training pipeline
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was invited to be part of something called the International Student Relations Seminar... it turned out only again in retrospect it was the training ground for future National Student Association officers and for CIA service... The National Student Association's International Program was funded by the CIA through a lot of subterfusions (Segments 203, 206, 207)

claims.claim: The CIA's NSA inner circle was made 'witting' through a formal protocol after officers were elected
claims.supporting_excerpt: then once you're in office, they take you out and they tell you that the CIA is bankrolling the international program. And they make you witty, witty, witty, w-i-t-t-i-n-g (Segments 225-226)
claims.rationale: Directly supported. Robinson's pronunciation 'witty' is a Whisper rendering of 'witting' which she herself spells out.

claims.claim: Karen Paget's book 'Patriotic Betrayal' documents the CIA-NSA relationship
claims.supporting_excerpt: That red and blue book, you see right there, what's it called, Patriotic Betrayal, Patriotic Betrayal? (Segment 211)
claims.rationale: Robinson holds up the book during the interview and identifies it by title. Note: Paget's book is the canonical reference.

claims.claim: Tim Jenkins, a Black NSA student leader, was steered from international vice-president into the domestic vice-president role because the CIA did not trust him
claims.supporting_excerpt: Tim Jenkins was active in National Student Association as a Howard student and his body president, and he has told me that he had hoped to be the vice chair of the vice president for international affairs and got sort of maneuvered over the domestic community. (Segment 229); Yes, they probably didn't trust him. They probably didn't trust him. And he's a black student too (Segment 230)
claims.rationale: Directly supported. Robinson's claim is based on Tim Jenkins's own account.

claims.claim: Robinson attended the 1962 World Festival of Youth and Students in Helsinki under CIA-funded auspices alongside Gloria Steinem
claims.supporting_excerpt: I also go to the world youth festival in Helsinki which is another piece... It's funded by the CIA she knows it's funded by the CIA (Segments 362, 364)

claims.claim: Robinson came to SNCC's Atlanta office in March 1964 and worked alongside Northern Coordinator Dinky Romilly
claims.supporting_excerpt: When I come to SNCC in March of 64, Dinky Romilly is the Northern Coordinator. We work together. (Segment 468)

claims.claim: She served the summer of 1964 as the lead Northern Coordinator in the Greenwood, Mississippi office during Freedom Summer, where SNCC relocated its national operations
claims.supporting_excerpt: I go to Mississippi for the summer. So I'm the lead Northern Coordinator in the Greenwood office during summer 64... this is part of SNCC kind of relocates the national office (Segments 470-471, 476)

claims.claim: The SNCC Atlanta office was on Raymond Street
claims.supporting_excerpt: every morning we come into the office at on Raymond Street (Segment 646)
claims.rationale: Directly supported and corroborated by ground-truth corpus James Forman entry ('the Atlanta national office at 8 1/2 Raymond Street').

claims.claim: SNCC field staff earned $10 a week or $9.64 after taxes
claims.supporting_excerpt: The pay that we received was $10 a week or $9.64 after taxes. (Segment 694)

claims.claim: Atlanta-based SNCC staff earned $40 a week to cover urban rent and utilities
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then when you lived in Atlanta, your pay was $40 a week. And that was to acknowledge the fact that you had to pay rent. (Segment 708-709)

claims.claim: SNCC used a WATS line (wide-area telephone line) to communicate with field projects daily
claims.supporting_excerpt: Someone would call all the projects, all the SNCC projects because we had a watch line, a wide area telephone line and it was kind of like an 800 number of today. (Segments 518-519)
claims.rationale: Directly supported. Note 'watch line' is Whisper's rendering of WATS line.

claims.claim: SNCC worked with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, SCLC, the Congress of Racial Equality, the National Lawyers Guild, and the Medical Committee for Human Rights
claims.supporting_excerpt: we were also working with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, with SCLC, with the Congress of Racial Equality, with the National Lawyers Guild, with the Medical Committee for Human Rights. (Segments 774-777)

claims.claim: In fall 1965 Robinson transitioned to federal-programs documentation work, traveling to Arkansas projects in Little Rock and Pine Bluff
claims.supporting_excerpt: in the fall of 65, after I had been the Northern Coordinator for a year, I decided I didn't want to do the Northern Coordinating anymore, but I wanted to do what was called federal programs... So I did go to Arkansas. I went to both the projects in Little Rock and Pine Bluff. (Segments 940-942, 955-956)

claims.claim: With Marion Barry, Robinson examined the U.S. Office of Education's files in Washington and found congressional dispensations from desegregation enforcement, publishing a report at a November 1965 press conference attended by John Lewis
claims.supporting_excerpt: Marion Barry, who was the head of the Washington DC office at the time, and I went to the office of education, and we asked if we could see their files... we documented all of that. We put it together in a report, which we published, it was a minute of graph... we had a big press conference in November, I think, of 65, maybe where John Lewis came (Segments 965, 977-979)

claims.claim: From Atlanta during the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack in March 1965, Robinson chartered a plane carrying Ivanhoe Donaldson, Courtland Cox, Prathia Hall, and a fourth person to Selma
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was able to charter a plane to send four people, Ivanhoe Donaldson, Courtland Cox, and Prathia Hall, and I don't know who the fourth person was, to Selma from Atlanta to get, so they could get there quickly, to be kind of the backup stick support for John Lewis and John Love (Segments 1066-1069)
claims.rationale: Directly supported and corroborated by ground-truth corpus Prathia Hall entry ('Robinson names her as one of four people chartered to Selma on a plane Robinson organized').

claims.claim: SNCC's Sojourner Motor Fleet consisted of approximately 20-40 cars purchased at dealer cost through United Auto Workers connections, equipped with CB radios for use in dangerous areas
claims.supporting_excerpt: we had a fleet of 40 cars called the Sojourner Motor Fleet... maybe it was 20 cars... we were able to buy a fleet of cars, very low end fords, and put CB radios in them so that people could communicate in dangerous, especially in dangerous areas... SNCC was able to buy the cars at cost, at what the price did. The dealer's price? Oh, because of the United Auto workers. (Segments 1074, 1078, 1083-1084, 1089-1091)
claims.rationale: Directly supported. Robinson herself notes uncertainty about fleet size ('40 cars or 20 cars'), which the summary preserves.

claims.claim: Robinson identifies Harry Belafonte as a likely SNCC funding source
claims.supporting_excerpt: I suspect it might have come from Harry Belafonte, or who knows... Harry Belafonte was one of SNCC's big benefactors. (Segments 1077, 1081)
claims.rationale: Robinson's speculation is identified as such in the summary.

claims.claim: Robinson attended the SNCC Thanksgiving 1963 meeting at Howard University, where freedom songs and 'We Shall Overcome' singing in a circle solidified her emotional commitment
claims.supporting_excerpt: can you have memories of that Thanksgiving meeting that SNCC held at Howard in 1963. The biggest memory I have of is the singing... standing in a circle singing singing all the freedom songs and then people with tears streaming down their eyes singing we shall overcome at the very end of the meeting (Segments 351-352)

claims.claim: Robinson collaborated as one of six editors of the 2010 book 'Hands on the Freedom Plow' with Judy Richardson, Dottie Zellner, Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescott Norman, and Jean Smith Young
claims.supporting_excerpt: this is 52 stories of women who were part of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee and it came out in 2010. So there were six of us who edited it. Judy Richardson, Dottie Zellner, Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescott Norman and Jean Smith Young and myself. (Segments 893-897)

claims.claim: After leaving SNCC in 1966, Robinson moved to Washington, DC, worked in a Black community arts program, and was involved in anti-war organizing
claims.supporting_excerpt: when I left SNCC, I moved to Washington and I stayed in Washington and I worked in a black community arts program for a number of years. And then I got involved in the anti-war movement (Segments 1374-1375)

claims.claim: In 1972 Robinson moved to Baltimore to work in a factory for eight years and married a Black factory worker
claims.supporting_excerpt: I moved to Baltimore in 1972 to work in a factory... So I worked in this factory for eight years. I married a factory worker, a black factory worker. (Segments 1386, 1389)

claims.claim: She worked 14 years at the Johns Hopkins School of Public Health in injury prevention and HIV/AIDS research
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went to work at Hopkins, Johns Hopkins in the School of Public Health. And I worked for 14 years in different, I worked in injury prevention and I worked in HIV and AIDS research. (Segment 1397)

claims.claim: Robinson became a regional resource person for Showing Up for Racial Justice (SURJ) after the April 2015 Baltimore uprising following Freddie Gray's death
claims.supporting_excerpt: in April when the Baltimore uprising happened, I was asked by the National Coordinator of SURJ... I was asked by SURJ if I wanted to become a regional resource person. And I agreed. (Segments 1354, 1362)
claims.rationale: Robinson does not explicitly name Freddie Gray in the transcript -- this is contextual inference from the well-documented April 2015 Baltimore uprising. The Baltimore uprising and SURJ regional resource role are both directly supported; the connection to Freddie Gray specifically is inferred.

claims.claim: Robinson identifies Hasan Kwame Jeffries and Emilye Crosby as young scholars writing important movement history
claims.supporting_excerpt: we have young scholars like Emilye Crosby and Hasan Kwame Jeffries and others who have written these powerful books and lifted up the history. (Segment 1432)

claims.claim: Septima Clark and Rosa Parks attended Highlander to talk about citizen education and organizing across the South
claims.supporting_excerpt: when the pre-the-siddings, I think they were already doing the Septima Clark for example and Rosa Parks went to Highlander to talk about citizen education and talk about organizing across the South. (Segment 1365)
claims.rationale: Directly supported by Robinson's testimony and consistent with the historical record.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's quality_metrics meet the 90/90 publication threshold and zero citation claims are flagged as unsupported. However, one citation claim ('Robinson became a regional resource person for SURJ after the April 2015 Baltimore uprising following Freddie Gray's death') is marked partially_supported because Robinson does not name Freddie Gray explicitly in the transcript -- the Freddie Gray connection is contextual inference from the well-documented April 2015 Baltimore uprising. Under the strict fail-closed gate (publishable = true iff partial == 0 AND unsupported == 0), this routes to human review. A reviewer can either (a) confirm the Freddie Gray contextual inference is appropriate institutional shorthand, in which case the claim can be promoted to supported and the summary published, or (b) revise the summary to say 'the April 2015 Baltimore uprising' without the Freddie Gray attribution, which would clear the gate. The conservative fail-closed routing is the correct outcome for a borderline contextual claim about a contemporary event.
